Facts.text: Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus on Rome's Capitoline Hill is dedicated on the Ides of September
Church of the Holy Sepulchre is consecrated in Jerusalem
General Belisarius of the Byzantine Empire defeats Gelimer and the Vandals at the Battle of Ad Decimium, near Carthage, North Africa
Sabinian begins his reign as Pope, replacing Gregory the Great
Duke Lotharius of Supplinburg is crowned German King Lothair III
Notorious serial killer Gilles de Rais is finally arrested upon an accusation brought against him by the Bishop of Nantes
Predro Álvares Cabral and his Portuguese expedition arrive in Calicut, India
Queen Isabella and King Ferdinand of Spain issue a Royal Warrant for the construction of a Royal Chapel (Capilla Real) to be built.
Pope Paul III signs Council of Bologna
English bishop Hugh Latimer arrested
Turkish fleet recaptures Tunis
Breda forms Union of Utrecht
Portuguese seize trading ship Rahimi and its 700 passengers, the largest Mughal trading ship, belonging to the Empress Mariam-uz-Zamani, leads to a check on Portuguese power
Sixteen rabbis, including Isaiah Horowitz, are imprisoned in Jerusalem
Admiral Tholen conquers 30+ Spanish frigates
First serious slave conspiracy in colonial America occurs in Virginia
Great Britain, Austria and Savoy-Sardinia sign the Treaty of Worms
Britain signs Austrian & Russian alliance
British beat French forces at the battle of Plains of Abrahams near Quebec - kills both leading generals Wolfe and Montcalm
Gotthold Lessing's "Die Juden," premieres in Frankfurt-am-Main
Prussian army moves into Netherlands
1st loan to US government (from NYC banks)
Curacao in British hands (until Jan 1803)
Battle of Jutas: Swedish forces under Lieutenant General Georg Carl von Döbeln defeat the Russians, making von Döbeln a Swedish war hero
Battle of Chapultepec: American forces capture Chapultepec Castle during the Mexican–American War, allowing US General Winfield Scott to take Mexico City
First US prizefight fatality (Tom McCoy)
The main-belt asteroid "12 Victoria" is discovered by J R Hind
Hamburg-US ship Austria catches fire & sinks, 471 die
1st naval battle of Civil War, Union frigate "Colorado" sinks privateer "Judah" off Pensacola, Fla
General Edward Canby orders South Carolina courts to impanel blacks jurors
Jay Gould and James Fisk attempt to control the US gold market
America Lewis Howard Latimer invents and patents electric lamp with a carbon filament
Battle at Count el-Kebir: British troops defeat Egyptian force in the decisive battle of the Anglo-Egytian War
Hugh Daily, a one-armed pitcher for Cleveland (Forest City), tosses a 1-0 no-hitter against Philadelphia
20,000 Paris construction workers go on strike
Mackinder, Ollier and Brocherel make the first ascent of Batian (5,199m - 17,058 ft), the highest peak of Mount Kenya
Filipino resistance fighters defeat a small American column in the Battle of Pulang Lupa, during the Philippine–American War
1st airplane flight in Europe, Brazilian Alberto Santos-Dumont test flies his hybrid 14-bis plane (attached to a hydrogen balloon for take-off), at Bagatelle, France
Canadian Interprovincial Rugby Football Union (IRFU) forms with merge of Hamilton Tigers, Toronto Argonauts (ORFU) and Ottawa Rough Riders, Montreal FC (QRFU)
Lusitania arrives in New York City after record 5 day crossing of Atlantic
Regina Rugby Club forms
Train accident at Weesp Netherlands, kills 42
Guy Bolton & George Middleton's "Adam & Eve," premieres in NYC
Battle of Sakarya, the defining battle of the Greco-Turkish War, ends after 21 days with Turkish forces holding back Greek troops near Ankara
Alleged world record temperature of 136.4°F (58°C) in El Aziziyah, Libya, in the shade (invalidated 2012 by the World Meteorological Organization)

Straw Hat Riot begins in New York City as youths aggressively taunt men wearing straw hats, getting an early jump on the unofficial season's end date of September 15; confrontations continue longer than usual, lasting eight days
The Great Fire of Smyrna: Anatolian city of Smyrna (Izmir) is set alight during the Turkish re-occupation of the city killing over 50,000
1st US University for African Americans, Xavier University, opens in New Orleans
MLB Brooklyn Robins pitcher Dazzy Vance no-hits the visiting Philadelphia Phillies, 10-1 at Ebbets Field
KOH-AM in Reno NV begins radio transmissions
San Felipe Segundo/Okeechobee hurricane makes landfall in Puerto Rico as a category 5 storm, killing over 300, leaving 500,000 homeless, and causing $50M in damages (about $790M in 2020 dollars)
Winnipeg Rugby Football Club 1st game, loses to St John's Rugby, 7-3
British RAF pilot George Stainworth flies world speed record (655 kph)
Right-radical coup of Dr Pfrimer fails in Austria
NY Yankees clinch their 7th AL pennant
Elizabeth McCombs becomes the first New Zealand female Member of Parliament
Rockslide near Whirlpool Rapids Bridge ends the Great Gorge and International Railway
Buckingham Palace damaged by German bombs
Italian troops under Marshal Graziani attack Egypt
Battle of Edson's Ridge (2nd Japanese assault) at Guadalcanal
Cubs shortstop Leonard Merullo makes 4 errors in 1 inning
German forces attack Stalingrad
German counterattack at Salerno
The Municipal Theatre of Corfu is destroyed during an aerial bombardment by Luftwaffe
30th Infantry division of US 1st Army frees Margraten
Great Atlantic hurricane reaches peak intensity as a Category 4 storm, goes on to kill 300-400 along the US East Coast
Last transport out camp Westerbork to Bergen Belsen
US 28th Infantry division opens assault on Siegfried line/Westwall
Boston Red Sox clinch AL pennant
WPVI TV channel 6 in Philadelphia, PA (ABC) begins broadcasting
Margaret Chase Smith, American politician (R-Me) elected senator, 1st woman to serve in both houses of US Congress
Ladies Pro Golf Association of America formed in NYC
St Louis Cards beat NY Giants 6-4 (rescheduled from 9/12) then at night lose to Boston Braves 2-0
Adolfo Ruiz Cortines elected president of Mexico
KGTV TV channel 10 in San Diego, CA (ABC/NBC) begins broadcasting
Pitcher Bob Trice becomes the first black player for Philadelphia Athletics
Cincinnati first baseman Ted Kluszewski scores a run in his record 17th consecutive game as Reds lose 5-4 to Pittsburgh Pirates at Forbes Field
WPBN TV channel 7 in Traverse City, MI (NBC) begins broadcasting
Swiss inventor George de Mestral is granted a patent for what would become known as Velcro
Dike around Dutch polder Eastern Flevoland closes
Stravinsky's "Canticum Sacrum," premieres in Venice
USSR's Luna 2 becomes 1st probe to contact another celestial body
Dutch 1st Chamber condemns soccer-law
Battles between UN & Katanga troops in Congo
Unmanned Mercury-Atlas 4 launched into Earth orbit
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
3rd sitting of 2nd Vatican council opens in Rome
St Louis is 1st NL to score runs in every game since 1923, win 15-2
WKEF TV channel 22 in Dayton, OH (NBC) begins broadcasting
Beatles win their 1st Grammy, for Best Group of 1964
Today Show's 1st totally color broadcast
Johannes Balthazar Vorster sworn in as premier of South Africa
Albania leaves Warsaw pact
Baltimore Orioles, win earliest AL Eastern division title
San Francisco GiantBobby Bondsis baseballs 4th 30-HR, 30-steal player
Palestine guerillas conquer Irbid Jordania
11 guards & 31 prisoners die in take over at Attica State Prison
Two North Ireland Loyalists are mortally injured when the bomb they were preparing exploded prematurely in a house in Bann Street, Belfast
WIIQ TV channel 41 in Demopolis, AL (PBS) begins broadcasting
World Hockey Association forms
ABC announces it obtained TV rights for 1976 Olympics
Syrian-Israeli dogfight over Mediterranean Sea
US Congress passes & sends a bill to President Nixon to lift NFL football's television blackout of sold out games
-17] 3 Japanese kidnap French ambassador in Hague
1st broadcast of "The Rockford Files" on NBC-TV
OPEC instructs its Secretary General to "carry out a study of supply and demand in relation to possible production controls"
Phillies set NL record, using 27 players in a game, St Louis uses 24, tying record of 51. Phils win 7-3 in 17
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
-27] Hurricane Eloise, kills 71 in the Caribbean & US
2nd Enterprise, approach & lands Test Cricket (ALT) flight (5m28s)
1st TV viewer discretion warning-Soap
2nd Test flight of Space Shuttle Enterprise
General Motors introduces 1st US diesel auto (Oldsmobile 88)
1st flight of McDonnell Douglas F-18A Hornet
New York Yankees recover from 14 games behind to gain sole possession of AL East 1st place with 7-3 win over the Tigers in Detroit
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
South Africa grants Venda independence (Not recognized out of South Africa)
1st United Negro College Fund
April Moon sets women's handbow distance record of 1,039 yds & 13"
Atlanta Falcons tie record of 31 points in 4th quarter (vs Green Bay)
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
50 die in Spantax Airlines DC-10 on takeoff from Malaga, Spain
Joe Lefevre gets 6 hits in one baseball game
MLB Oakland A's outfielderRickey Henderson3rd straight 100 steals for season
Steve O'Shaughnessy scores 100 in 35 mins, Lancs v Leics
US mint strikes 1st gold coin in 50 years (Olympic Eagle)
Shimon Peresforms Israeli government with Likud
STS 41-G launch vehicle moves to launch pad
John Williams introduces new Today Show theme
Bert Blyleven gives up a record 44 HRs in a season
Cesium-137 stolen from abandoned hospital in Rio de Janeiro
Paul Lynch of Great Britain does 32,573 push-ups in 24 hours
Tampa Bay quarterback Steve DeBerg establishes a franchise record with 5 touchdown passes in the Buccaneers 48-10 win over the Atlanta Falcons
10th time, 4 players hit baseball major-league record grand slams
9 hard/software manufacturers announce EISA computer bus in NY
Gilbert is measured the strongest (26.13 barometer) hurricane ever in the Western Hemisphere
Commuter train at Johannesburg South Africa attacked, 36 die
Iraqi troops storm residence of French ambassador in Kuwait
Robert E Nederlander appointed NY Yankee managing general partner
US Senate Judiciary Comittee opens hearing on confirmation of David Souter for a seat on the Supreme Court
55 ton concrete beam falls in Montreal's Olympic Stadium
Kim Zmeskal is 1st American to win a medal at World Gymnastics Championships, she wins the gold with 39.848 pts
NY Giants trailing Dallas Cowboys 34-0 in 3rd, lose 34-28
Queens, NYC begins required recycling
Wang Junxia runs a new women's 3000m world record (8:06.11) 1 day after setting previous record at the Chinese National Games
George Burns undergoes surgery to drain fluid from his brain
Space probe Ulysses passes the south pole of the Sun
The Kuwaiti Oil Ministry states its intention to seek a 200-million-barrels-per-day increase to its current 2-million-barrels-per-day crude oil production quota at OPEC meeting in Vienna
Phil Simmons scores 171 & takes 6-14 for Leics v Durham
Carolina Hurricanes' first exhibition game, defeat NY Islanders 4-1
Elton Johnreleases single "Something About the Way You Look Tonight" in the UK
50th Emmy Awards: The Practice, Frasier, Andre Braugher & Christine Lahti win
Bomb explodes in Moscow, Russia, killing at least 119 people
Relative calm returns to world oil markets as US retail gasoline prices return to normal levels; energy trading by Houston energy companies resumes and limited commercial aviation starts
Construction begins onHadrian's Wall in Northern England
Francis of Assisiis afflicted with stigmata after a vision while praying on Mount La Verna
First day of two-day Battle of Marignano:Francis I's French army defeats Swiss army force near Milan
France's KingLouis XVIaccepts constitution
English chemistMichael Faradaydiscovers the "Faraday effect," the influence of a magnetic field on polarized light
Cecil Rhodes' colonists hoist the Union Jack in at Fort Salisbury in Mashonaland (modern day Harare Zimbabwe)
Chicago Cubs infielders Joe Tinker, Johnny Evers, &Frank Chanceappear together as teammates for 1st time, creating the famed double-play combination of "Tinker to Evers to Chance"
Ty Cobbclinches AL HR title with his 9th HR (all inside-the-park)
Miguel Primo de Riveraleads a military coup with the support of KingAlfonso XIIIto become dictator and Prime Minister of Spain
NY Yankees clinch AL pennant after 5-3 win over Cleveland Indians at Yankee Stadium;Babe Ruthhits HR #52 en route to MLB record 60
Paavo Nurmiruns world record 20,000m (1:04:38.4)
17-year-old Cleveland Indians future Baseball HOF pitcherBob Fellerstrikes out then record 17 Philadelphia A's in 5-2 win at League Park, Cleveland
Acting on the orders ofLouis Buchalter, Murder Inc. killers gun down Joseph Rosen, a Brooklyn candy store owner
Alexander Cartwrightis inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
Amon Göthremoved as head of Kraków-Płaszów concentration camp by the SS for stealing state property
T-Bone Walkerrecords his biggest hit "Call it Stormy Monday (But Tuesday Is Just as Bad)" in Hollywood, California

Italian Ferrari driverAlberto Ascariretains Formula 1 World Drivers Championship although forced to retire (engine trouble) in season ending Italian Grand Prix at Monza; wins title by 6.5 points fromJuan Manuel Fangio
BravesWarren Spahnis 1st lefty to win 20 or more games 9 times
Queen Julianain a gala ceremony christens passenger ship SS Rotterdam, the last 'ship of state'
14-year-oldPriscillaBeaulieu meets 24-year-oldElvis Presleyat a party at his home in Bad Nauheim, Germany
Jim Bouton's 20th win clinches New York Yankees' 28th pennant
Beatles release single "Yesterday" - just Paul, with strings arranged byGeorge Martin
Willie Mays's 500th HR (off Don Nottebart), Giants 11th straight win
"Scooby-Doo Where are You" byHanna-Barberadebuts on CBS in the US
Plastic Ono Band's 1st live performance, featuringJohn Lennon,Yoko OnoandEric Clapton, at the Toronto Rock and Roll Revival festival
Frank Robinsonhits his 500th HR
Nikita Khrushchev, Soviet premier, buried in Moscow
American soul singers "The Pips" appear, withoutGladys Knight, on ABC-TV's "TheRichard PryorShow"
33rd Emmy Awards: "Taxi", "Hill Street Blues";Judd Hirschand Isabel Sanford win

MLB PhilliesSteve Carltontosses a complete-game shutout and hits a home run for the 4th time in his career, in 2-0 win over St. Louis, at Veteran's Stadium, Philadelphia; he becomes only pitcher to do so in three different decades
Dan Quisenberryrecord 39th season save
Shimon Peresbecomes the 8th Prime Minister of Israel, succeedingYitzhak Shamir
Super Mario Bros game first appears, created byShigeru Miyamotoat Nintendo
ArchbishopDesmond Tutuleads biggest anti-apartheid protest march in South Africa
Fay Vincentelected Major League Baseball's 8th commissioner
"Law and Order" created byDick Wolffirst premieres on NBC
Joe Carter1st baseball player with 3 consecutive 100 RBI seasons with 3 different teams (Indians, Padres, Blue Jays)
Public unveiling of the Oslo Accords, an Israeli-Palestinian peace agreement initiated by Norway, signed by Israeli Minister of Foreign AffairsShimon Peresand PLO officialMahmoud Abbas
"Ready To Die" debut studio album byThe Notorious B.I.G.released
Elton Johnreleases single "Candle in the Wind 1997", a tribute toDiana, Princess of Walesin the UK
Mother Teresa's State Funeral held in India
John Elway's #7 jersey is retired by the Denver Broncos
Michelangelobegins work on his statue of David, a masterpiece of Renaissance sculpture

The Grand Assault on Gibraltar by the allied French and Spanish fleets, including ten floating batteries and land forces, is heavily defeated by the British garrison, with all floating batteries destroyed and allied casualties of 1,473 compared to only 83 for the British
John II Komnenos, Emperor of the Byzantine Empire (1118-43), born in Constantinople, Byzantine Empire (d. 1143)
Alexander Neckam, English theologist and encyclopedist, born in St. Albans, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1217)
Cesare Borgia, Duke of Valentinois, Italian aristocrat, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1507)
John Leland, English poet and antiquarian, born in London (d. 1552)
William Cecil, 1st baron Burghley, English statesman, born in Bourne, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1598)
Pandolfo Zallamella, Italian composer (Ingrediente Domino), born in Ravenna, Papal States (d. 1590)
Francesco Manelli, Roman Baroque composer, born in Tivoli, Papal States (d. 1667)
William Brereton, 1st Baronet, English soldier and politician, born in Brereton Hall, Cheshire, England (d. 1661)
Hercule Brehy, Flemish composer and organist, born in Brussels, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1737)
Élisabeth Charlotte d'Orléans, Duchess of Lorraine, born in Château de Saint-Cloud, France (d. 1744)
Theodor Christlieb Reinhold, German composer, born in Eppendorf, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1755)
Luca Antonio Predieri, Italian composer and violinist, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1767)
Giuseppe Sigismondo, Italian historian and composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1826)
Henry Kobell, Dutch painter and cartoonist, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1779)
Oliver Evans, American inventor who pioneered high-pressure steam engine, born in Newport, Delaware (d. 1819)
Samuel Wilson, American meat packer and possible namesake of Uncle Sam, born in Menotomy, Province of Massachusetts Bay (d. 1854)
Arnold Ruge, German political philosopher and writer, born in Bergen auf Rügen, Germany (d. 1880)
Joseph L. Hogg, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Morgan County, Georgia (d. 1862)
Moritz Ganz, German composer and cellist, born in Mainz, Grand Duchy of Hesse, Confederation of the Rhine (d. 1868)

John Sedgwick, American Major General (Union Army), who is best remembered for his famous last words "they couldn't hit an elephant at this distance" before being shot in battle, born in Cornwall, Connecticut (d. 1864)
Nicolas Beets [Hildebrand], Dutch writer (Camera Obscura) and theologian, born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1903)
John M. Palmer, American politician (U.S. Senator from Illinois) and American Civil War General (Union), born in Eagle Creek, Kentucky (d. 1900)
Gustave Aimard, French world explorer and writer (Grande Flibuste), born in Paris (d. 1883)
Marie von Ebner-Eschenbach, Austrian writer (The Child of the Parish), born in Zdislavič, Moravia (d. 1916)
Andrew Noble, Scottish physicist who was a founder of the science of ballistics, born in Greenock (d. 1915)
John McCausland, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1927)
Johannes de Koo, Dutch journalist and playwright, born in Middelie, Netherlands (d. 1909)
John H. Bankhead, U.S. Senator from Alabama, born in Moscow, Marion County, Alabama (d. 1920)
Ödön Mihalovich, Hungarian composer, born in Fericsánci, Slavonia (d. 1929)
Richard Kiepert, German cartographer (Africa), born in Weimar, Germany (d. 1915)
Charles Dupee Blake, American composer, born in Walpole, Massachusetts (d. 1903)
Walter Reed, U.S. Army physician and bacteriologist who proved mosquitoes transmit yellow fever, born in Belroi, Virginia (d. 1902)

Sophia Perovskaya, Soviet icon, aristocrat, and socialist revolutionary (Narodnaya Volya), who helped orchestrate the successful assassination of Alexander II of Russia, born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1881)
Jacobus van Looy, Dutch writer (Jaapje; De Dood van mijn Poes (The Death of My Cat) ) and painter (Witte poes in een open raam (White Cat in an Open Window)), born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1930)
Michał Drzymała, Polish peasant rebel (Drzymała's wagon), born in Province of Posen, Poland (d. 1937)
Catherinus Elling, Norwegian organist, composer, educator, and ethnomusicologist, born in Christiania, Norway (d. 1942)
Dirk Bos, Dutch Liberal 2nd Member of Parliament, born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1916)
Arthur Henderson, British Labour minister (Nobel Peace Prize 1934), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1935)
Willis Cuttell, English cricket spin bowler (2 Tests, 6 wickets; Lancashire CCC), born in Sheffield, England (d. 1929)
Adolf Meyer, American psychiatrist and neurologist (pioneered mental hygiene), born in Niederweningen, Switzerland (d. 1950)
Alma Kruger, American actress (Made For Each Other), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1960)
Henry F. Ashurst, American Democratic politician (U.S. Senator from Arizona), born in Winnemucca, Nevada (d. 1962)
Sherwood Anderson, American author and publisher (Winesburg Ohio), born in Camden, Ohio (d. 1941)
Stanley Lord, Captain of the SS Californian the night of the Titanic disaster, born in Bolton, Lancashire, England (d. 1962)
Wilhelm Filchner, German explorer who led the German Antarctic expedition of 1911–12, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1957)
Jesse L. Lasky, American film producer (The Dictator, The Squaw Man), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1958)
Henri Potiron, French composer, born in Rezé-lès-Nantes, Loire-Atlantique, France (d. 1972)
Ramón Grau, 6th and 10th President of Cuba, born in La Palma, Spanish Cuba (d. 1969)
Wilhelm Blaschke, Austrian integral geometer, born in Graz, Austria (d. 1962)

Robert Robinson, British chemist (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1947 for investigation of plant lipids), born in Rufford, England (d. 1975)[1]
Leopold Ružička, Croatian-Swiss chemist (Nobel 1939), born in Vukovar, Croatia (d. 1976)
Pierre Reverdy, French poet (Nord-Sud), born in Occitanie (d. 1960)

J. B. Priestly, English novelist and scriptwriter (The Good Companions, An Inspector Calls), born in Bradford, United Kingdom (d. 1984)
Julian Tuwim, Polish poet and lyricist, born in Łódź, Congress Poland (d. 1953)
Ruth McDevitt, American actress (All in the Family), born in Coldwater, Michigan (d. 1976)
Morris Kirksey, American athlete (Olympic gold 100m, 4 x 100m, men's rugby 1920), born in Waxahachie, Texas (d. 1981)
Tadeusz Szeligowski, Polish composer (The Peacock and the Girl), born in Lemberg, Austro-Hungarian Galicia (d. 1963)
Gaspar Cassadó, Spanish cellist and composer, born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1966)
Anton Constandse, Dutch anarchist and writer, born in Brouwershaven, Netherlands (d. 1985)

Frank "Big Boy" Goudie, American jazz tenor saxophonist and clarinetist, and upholsterer, born in Youngsville, Louisiana (d. 1964)
Gladys George, American actress (Roaring Twenties), born in Patten, Maine (d. 1954)
Arthur Mitchell, English cricket batsman (6 Tests, 2 x 50; Yorkshire CCC), born in Baildon, England (d. 1976)

(Leon) "Chu" Berry, American swing jazz tenor saxophonist (Fletcher Henderson; Cab Calloway), born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1941)

Charles Brown, American blues singer and pianist ("Merry Christmas, Baby"), born in Texas City (d. 1999)
Yma Sumac [Chavarri], Peruvian-American 5 octave soprano (Omar Khayyam), born in Ichocan, Cajamarca, Peru (d. 2008)

Scott Brady [Gerald Tierney], American actor (Shotgun Slade, China Syndrome, Gremlins, Johnny Guitar), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1985)
Gabriel Charpentier, Canadian composer, and poet, born in Richmond, Quebec (d. 2019)
Mel Tormé, American jazz singer, known as 'The Velvet Fog' and composer (The Christmas Song), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1999)
Emile Francis, Canadian Hockey HOF coach (NY Rangers 1965-75; St. Louis Blues) and executive (GM: NY Rangers, St. Louis Blues, Hartford Whalers), born in North Battleford, Saskatchewan (d. 2022)[1]
Samuel Smith, American chemist and co-inventor of Scotchgard, born in New York City (d. 2005)[1]
Ernest L. Boyer, American educator and chancellor (NY's State Universities-SUNY), born in Dayton, Ohio (d. 1995)
Nicolai Ghiaurov, Bulgarian operatic basso singer, born in Velingrad, Bulgaria (d. 2004)
Jimmy McLane, American swimmer (Olympic gold 1500m 1948), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2020)
Robert Gavron, Baron Gavron, English publisher and multi-millionaire (St Ives Group), born in London (d. 2015)
Adrienne Kennedy, American educator and playwright (Ohio State Murders, Funnyhouse of a Negro), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Barbara Bain, American actress (Cinnamon-Mission Impossible, Space 1999), born in Chicago, Illinois
Marjorie Jackson, Australian sprinter (Olympic gold 1952) and 33rd Governor of South Australia, born in Coffs Harbour, Australia
Bengt Hallberg, Swedish jazz pianist and composer, born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 2013)
Bronius Kutavičius, Lithuanian composer (The Gates of Jerusalem; Lokys) and teacher (Čiurlionis School of Art, 1975-2000), born in Molainiai, Lithuania (d. 2021)
Eileen Fulton [Margaret McLarty], American actress (As the World Turns, 1960-2010; Our Private World), mystery novelist, and cabaret singer, born in Asheville, North Carolina (d. 2025)
Bill Woolsey, American swimmer (Olympic gold men's 4×200m freestyle relay 1952, silver 1956), born in Honolulu, Hawaii (d. 2022)
Stefano Delle Chiaie, Italian neo-fascist terrorist (founder of Avanguardia Nazionale), born in Caserta, Italy (d. 2019)
Don Bluth, American animator and film director (The Secret of NIMH), born in El Paso, Texas
Fred Silverman, American broadcasting executive and producer (ABC, NBC and CBS), born in New York City (d. 2020)
Judith Martin, American author, journalist and columnist (Miss Manners), born in Washington, D.C.
Bill Janklow, American politician and four-term Governor of South Dakota (Republican: 1979-87, 1995-2003), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Elaine Delmar (neé Hutchinson), British jazz and cabaret singer and stage actress, born in Harpenden, Hertfordshire, England [some sources cite birthday as 9/01]
Gene Page, American arranger and composer (Righteous Brothers; Marvin Gaye; Whitney Houston), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1998)
Larry Speakes, Acting White House Press Secretary under President Ronald Reagan, born in Cleveland, Mississippi (d. 2014)
Richard Kiel, American actor (Jaws in "Moonraker"; Skidoo; So Fine), born in Detroit, Michigan, (d. 2014)
(Lionel) "Linc" Chamberland, American R&B and jazz-fusion guitarist, born in Norwalk, Connecticut (d. 1987)
Joyce Schouten, Dutch pop singer (Fouryo's - "Dance With Me One More Time"), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands
Óscar Arias Sánchez, 40th & 45th President of Costa Rica (Nobel Peace Prize 1987), born in Heredia, Costa Rica
